description: This figure illustrates the multiphase heterogeneous microstructure design and mechanical property relationships in a low-density steel (Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al-0.4Si) subjected to intercritical annealing at 750°C, 800°C, and 850°C followed by low bainite aging at 400°C. The image displays scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs and electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) maps revealing four distinct phases: polygonal ferrite (F, grain size 4.53-5.35 μm), lath-like bainite/tempered martensite (B/TM), fresh martensite (FM), and retained austenite (RA, volume fraction 0.6-7.4%). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images show carbide distributions within bainite/martensite laths—oriented at 60° to boundaries in bainite and randomly in tempered martensite—along with geometrically necessary dislocation (GND) pile-ups at phase interfaces. Digital image correlation (DIC) results demonstrate strain partitioning between soft ferrite and hard domains, with sample 750T showing uniform deformation while sample 850T exhibits localized strain concentration. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns confirm BCC and FCC phase constituents. The figure supports the core conclusion that this hierarchical microstructure achieves superior strength-ductility combinations (yield strength 697-991 MPa, tensile strength 1068-1295 MPa, elongation 13.9-34.0%) via back stress strengthening (236-300 MPa), TRIP effects from retained austenite transformation, and coordinated deformation mechanisms that mitigate stress concentrations at phase boundaries.

The figure presents the complete thermomechanical processing route and sample preparation for Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al-0.4Si low-density steel. The first panel displays a temperature-time profile showing: homogenization at 1200°C for 60 min, multi-pass hot rolling from 1100°C to 880°C with 90% reduction, cold rolling with 60% reduction to 1.6 mm thickness, intercritical annealing at 750/800/850°C for 30 min (between Ac1 and Ac3 temperatures), and optional low bainite aging at 400°C for 30 min. Microstructural evolution is illustrated through optical/SEM images at key stages: hot-rolled (banded ferrite-pearlite), cold-rolled (elongated grains), annealed (multiphase with retained austenite), and tempered (carbide precipitation). The second panel shows photographs of the thin-wall tubular sample with RD (rolling direction) marked, alongside detailed tensile specimen geometry (gauge length 14 mm, width 5 mm, thickness 1 mm, total length 58 mm, grip width 10 mm) machined from the 1.6 mm thick sheet, demonstrating the sample preparation for mechanical testing.

This figure presents scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images showing the multiphase microstructure evolution of Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al-0.4Si low-density steel during rolling and heat treatment processes. The eight subfigures display: (a) hot-rolled (HR) state showing lath-like fresh martensite (FM) and bainite (B); (b) cold-rolled state with elongated lamellar structure containing FM and B; (c-e) intercritical annealing at 750°C (AN750), 800°C (AN800), and 850°C (AN850) revealing polygonal ferrite (F), bainite (B), fresh martensite (FM), and retained austenite (RA, indicated by red arrows); and (f-h) corresponding annealed samples with additional low bainite aging at 400°C (AN750-T, AN800-T, AN850-T) showing tempered martensite (TM) formation alongside FM, F, and B. The images, captured at magnifications indicated by 5-10 μm scale bars, demonstrate the heterogeneous microstructure evolution from deformed lath structures to multiphase mixtures, with phase fractions and morphologies varying systematically with annealing temperature. The coordinate system indicates normal direction (ND) and rolling direction (RD).

Figure 3 illustrates the evolution of retained austenite (RA) during thermomechanical processing of a low-density Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al-0.4Si steel. Panels (a)-(d) display EBSD phase maps where the BCC phase (ferrite/martensite) appears red and the FCC phase (austenite) appears green. The hot-rolled sample HR (a) shows 100% BCC phase with zero RA fraction. After intercritical annealing at 750°C, sample AN750 (b) exhibits 3.0 vol% RA with an average diameter of 0.59 μm. As annealing temperature increases to 800°C (AN800, c) and 850°C (AN850, d), RA content decreases to 1.1 vol% and 0.2 vol% respectively, while the average diameter increases to 0.72 μm and 0.83 μm. Panel (e) presents a line chart summarizing this quantitative trend, confirming that RA content peaks at 750°C. Panel (f) shows XRD patterns verifying the coexistence of BCC (α) and FCC (γ) phases, with the γ peaks progressively diminishing at higher annealing temperatures. These results demonstrate that annealing at 750°C optimally stabilizes RA, which is essential for activating the TRIP effect and enhancing mechanical properties.

This figure presents comprehensive transmission electron microscopy (TEM) characterization of a low-density Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al-0.4Si steel processed via intercritical annealing at 750°C, 800°C, and 850°C followed by tempering at 400°C (samples AN750-T, AN800-T, AN850-T). The bright-field and dark-field TEM images (scale bars ranging from 100 nm to 2 μm) reveal the multiphase microstructure comprising ferrite (F), bainite (B), tempered martensite (TM), fresh martensite (FM), and retained austenite (RA). Key microstructural features include the distinction between B and TM based on carbide orientation—carbides in B align at approximately 60° to boundaries while randomly distributed in TM—and the identification of RA films between laths. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern confirms the [110]γ zone axis of austenite. XRD patterns verify the presence of α-ferrite and γ-austenite phases across all three samples. The figure demonstrates that TEM successfully differentiates B from TM (which EBSD cannot distinguish), revealing that RA content decreases with increasing annealing temperature from 7.4% (AN750-T) to 0.6% (AN850-T), supporting the heterogeneous multiphase structure design for strength-ductility optimization.

Figure 5 presents comprehensive EBSD-based phase separation and interfacial strain analysis for low-density steel samples AN750-T, AN800-T, and AN850-T processed at different intercritical annealing temperatures (750°C, 800°C, and 850°C) followed by tempering. The combined Image Quality (IQ) and phase distribution maps display the spatial arrangement of FCC retained austenite (green, fractions 0.074/0.024/0.006) and BCC phases (red, fractions 0.926/0.976/0.994), revealing decreasing retained austenite content with increasing annealing temperature. The IQ histograms exhibit distinct bimodal distributions where Peak I (~4-5×10⁴) corresponds to bainite/tempered martensite (B/TM) and Peak II (~7-9×10⁴) to fresh martensite (FM), enabling phase discrimination based on Kikuchi pattern band contrast differences arising from carbon concentration and dislocation density variations. Kernel Average Misorientation (KAM) maps visualize strain localization at phase boundaries, with point-to-origin profiles demonstrating that KAM values peak at ferrite/B/TM interfaces then gradually decrease, indicating geometrically necessary dislocation accumulation coordinating deformation between soft and hard domains. Notably, AN800-T exhibits the highest average KAM value (0.69°) compared to AN750-T (0.53°) and AN850-T (0.62°), attributed to its elevated bainite/martensite content, confirming significant strain partitioning and back stress generation at heterophase interfaces.

This figure comprises four sub-panels presenting the microstructural characteristics and mechanical properties of multiphase low-density steels (Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al-0.4Si) subjected to intercritical annealing at 750°C, 800°C, and 850°C followed by low-temperature bainitic aging at 400°C (designated AN750-T, AN800-T, and AN850-T). Panel (a) displays bar charts of volume fractions for four distinct phases: ferrite (F), bainite/tempered martensite (B/TM), fresh martensite (FM), and retained austenite (RA). As annealing temperature increases from 750°C to 850°C, RA content drastically decreases from 7.4% to 0.6%, FM reduces from 8.3% to 3.3%, while F fraction initially decreases then increases (49.3% → 46.8% → 54.7%). Panel (b) shows the corresponding average grain/phase diameters: F coarsens from 4.53 μm to 5.35 μm, whereas B/TM refines significantly to 0.86 μm at 850°C. Panel (c) presents engineering stress-strain curves, revealing that AN800-T achieves the highest ultimate tensile strength (1295 MPa) with 18.7% elongation, while AN750-T exhibits superior ductility (34.0% elongation) with tensile strength of 1068 MPa; AN850-T shows reduced elongation (13.9%) despite high strength (1269 MPa). Panel (d) compares the strength-ductility balance against conventional advanced high-strength steels (QP, DP, TRIP, TBF), demonstrating that the present steel achieves an outstanding product of strength and elongation (PSE) exceeding 30 GPa·%, positioning it above typical performance envelopes. The figure collectively establishes that the heterogeneous multiphase microstructure, particularly the optimized retention of RA (7.4%) and refined B/TM domains in AN750-T, enables effective strain partitioning and back-stress strengthening, delivering exceptional mechanical properties through the TRIP effect and geometrically necessary dislocation accumulation at soft-hard phase boundaries.

Figure 7 presents three line graphs showing the calculated evolution of deformation-induced martensite (DIM) and retained austenite (RA) volume fractions versus true strain for a Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al-0.4Si low-density steel processed via intercritical annealing at 750°C, 800°C, and 850°C (followed by tempering at 400°C). Based on the Olson-Cohen (O-C) kinetic model with fitting parameters α=5, β=1.28, and n=0.55, each sub-plot displays dual-axis curves: black lines (left y-axis) represent the increasing DIM fraction, while red lines (right y-axis) depict the decreasing RA fraction during deformation. The three panels correspond to samples with initial RA contents of approximately 7.4%, 2.4%, and 0.6%, respectively, reaching final DIM fractions of 4.74%, 1.16%, and 0.28% at the indicated strains. The figure quantifies the TRIP effect kinetics, demonstrating that strain-induced martensitic transformation diminishes with higher annealing temperatures due to reduced RA stability and content.

This figure presents EPMA (Electron Probe Microanalyzer) elemental mapping and corresponding line-scan composition profiles for Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al-0.4Si low-density steel subjected to intercritical annealing at 750°C, 800°C, and 850°C followed by tempering (samples AN750-T, AN800-T, and AN850-T). The top row displays two-dimensional distribution maps of Fe, C, Mn (labeled as M), Al, and Si, where color contrast indicates elemental concentration (blue representing lower concentration and green/yellow higher concentration). The bottom row shows quantitative line-scan data extracted along the red dashed arrows indicated in the carbon maps, plotting atomic percentages of Si, Al, Mn, and C as functions of distance. The shaded regions in line scans correspond to specific phases: B/TM (bainite/tempered martensite), RA (retained austenite), and FM (fresh martensite). The data demonstrate that carbon is primarily enriched in the harder phases (B/TM, FM, and RA) while being depleted in ferrite. With increasing annealing temperature from 750°C to 850°C, the carbon distribution becomes progressively more uniform across the microstructure, whereas Mn, Al, and Si show relatively homogeneous distributions regardless of temperature. These results support the quantitative phase composition strategy by revealing the elemental partitioning behavior critical for understanding phase stability and mechanical properties.

This figure presents three true stress-strain curves comparing experimental mechanical behavior with iso-work model predictions for multiphase low-density steels (Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al-0.4Si) subjected to different intercritical annealing temperatures (750°C, 800°C, and 850°C) followed by low bainite aging at 400°C (designated AN750-T, AN800-T, and AN850-T). Each subplot displays experimental flow stress (red solid line), iso-work model calculations (black dashed line), and the back stress contribution (σ_B, shaded gray region), alongside digital image correlation (DIC) strain field maps showing spatial strain evolution. As annealing temperature increases, the deviation between experimental and modeled curves grows, indicating enhanced back stress hardening (σ_B = 217, 300, and 278 MPa at necking, respectively). The DIC sequences reveal distinct deformation mechanisms: AN750-T exhibits uniform strain distribution with stress partitioning during uniform plastic deformation; AN800-T shows two local strain concentration points that expand at 45° and coalesce leading to necking; while AN850-T displays strain concentration in hard domains. These observations demonstrate that heterogeneous microstructures comprising ferrite, bainite/tempered martensite, and retained austenite achieve strength-ductility synergy through back stress strengthening and coordinated strain partitioning between soft and hard domains.

This figure presents comprehensive TEM microstructural analysis of multiphase low-density steel samples (AN750-T, AN800-T, AN850-T) processed via intercritical annealing at 750–850 °C followed by tempering at 400 °C. Panel (a) displays bright-field TEM images revealing heterogeneous distributions of soft domains (ferrite, F) and hard domains (bainite/tempered martensite, B/TM; fresh martensite, FM) with geometrically necessary dislocations (GNDs) accumulating at phase boundaries. Key heterostructure parameters are marked: R (average characteristic size of back stress influence zone) and Δh (average thickness of heterogeneous layers). Panel (b) shows a dark-field image of AN850-T highlighting dense dislocation regions (yellow triangles). Additional micrographs illustrate dislocation pile-ups (yellow T-symbols) at interfaces. Accompanying stress-strain curves (depicted as filled area charts) decompose the true stress into contributions from back stress (σ_B), ferrite (f_Fσ_F), bainite/tempered martensite (f_BTMσ_BTM), deformation-induced martensite (f_DIMσ_DIM), retained austenite (f_RAσ_RA), and fresh martensite (f_FMσ_FM), demonstrating that strain gradients at soft/hard domain interfaces generate significant back stress hardening (up to ~300 MPa) that coordinates deformation and delays necking.

This figure presents SEM characterization of crack paths and dimple statistics in multiphase low-density steels (Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al-0.4Si) processed under three intercritical annealing temperatures with subsequent tempering: AN750-T (750°C), AN800-T (800°C), and AN850-T (850°C). Panel (a) shows microcrack initiation and propagation behavior, where microscale voids (yellow dashed circles) nucleate predominantly at soft/hard domain interfaces. In AN750-T and AN800-T, crack tips blunt at hard domain interfaces generating secondary cracks (yellow arrows), whereas in AN850-T the hard domains are severed (red arrows) due to increased brittleness from carbon and manganese migration. Panel (b) reveals the central fracture zone morphology: AN750-T exhibits deep equiaxed dimples indicating ductile fracture, while AN800-T and AN850-T display mixed-mode fracture with quasi-cleavage facets (red dashed outlines) originating from fresh martensite alongside dimples (yellow arrows). Panel (c) presents bimodal dimple size distribution statistics analyzed via Nano Measure software, showing average dimple sizes of 1.54 μm (AN750-T), 1.44 μm (AN800-T), and 1.31 μm (AN850-T), with Peak I decreasing from 0.72 to 0.60 μm and Peak II decreasing from 2.26 to 1.78 μm as annealing temperature increases. The progressive reduction in dimple size and emergence of quasi-cleavage features at higher temperatures indicate that local oversaturation of alloying elements in hard domains (bainite/tempered martensite and fresh martensite) increases strength but compromises toughness, diminishing the capacity for stress concentration alleviation through multiphase deformation compatibility.

This figure presents EBSD analysis of the necking region after tensile fracture for low-density steel samples AN750-T, AN800-T, and AN850-T (intercritically annealed at 750°C, 800°C, and 850°C respectively, followed by low bainite aging at 400°C). Panel (a) shows Kikuchi pattern quality overlaid with phase distribution maps, where red indicates retained austenite with measured volume fractions decreasing from 0.018 (AN750-T) to 0.004 (AN800-T) and 0.002 (AN850-T). Panel (b) displays Kikuchi pattern quality combined with Kernel Average Misorientation (KAM) maps (scale 0-5), highlighting strain localization in hard regions (bainite/tempered martensite and fresh martensite) versus soft ferrite (F) domains; red ellipses mark stress concentration sites and white pixels indicate regions prone to micropore nucleation. Panel (c) provides quantitative line profiles across phase boundaries (yellow arrows in (a)), showing point-to-point (P-T-P) misorientation (red curves, left axis, °) and image quality values (black curves, right axis, ×10⁻⁴). The P-T-P misorientation averages are 3.50°, 4.48°, and 3.50° respectively. Sharp spikes in misorientation (up to ~55°) coinciding with minima in pattern quality at phase interfaces demonstrate significant strain accumulation and geometrically necessary dislocation pile-up at soft/hard domain boundaries, evidencing the hetero-deformation induced stress partitioning mechanism.

The figure comprises two panels detailing thermal analysis for Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al-0.4Si low-density steel. The left schematic depicts the dilatometry temperature profile: initial rapid heating at 10°C/s to 200°C, subsequent slow heating at 0.01°C/s to 980°C with a 300 s isothermal hold, and final rapid cooling at -30°C/s. The right panel presents the dilatometry curve showing relative length change (ΔL) versus temperature, from which critical phase transformation temperatures are determined: Ac1 = 705°C (austenite start), Ac3 = 950°C (austenite finish), Bf = 380°C (bainite finish), and Ms = 346°C (martensite start). The thermal expansion curve exhibits slope inflections corresponding to phase transformations during heating and cooling. These measured critical temperatures define the intercritical annealing range (705-950°C) employed in subsequent processing at 750-850°C and validate the heat treatment window for optimizing multiphase microstructure formation.

This figure presents electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) orientation imaging and statistical analysis of deformation-induced martensite (DIM) in a low-density steel (Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al-0.4Si) subjected to intercritical annealing at 750°C (AN750-T), 800°C (AN800-T), and 850°C (AN850-T) followed by low bainite aging at 400°C. Subfigures (a) and (b) display inverse pole figure (IPF) maps showing microstructural morphology before and after parent grain reconstruction, respectively, revealing multiphase microstructures consisting of ferrite, bainite/tempered martensite, fresh martensite, and retained austenite. Subfigure (c) presents the equivalent circle diameter distributions of DIM, demonstrating that the average DIM size increases with annealing temperature from 0.12 μm (AN750-T) to 0.15 μm (AN800-T) and 0.19 μm (AN850-T). The results indicate that higher annealing temperatures promote coarser microstructures and larger DIM sizes, correlating with reduced retained austenite content and altered strain partitioning behavior between soft ferrite and hard bainite/martensite domains.
